lesson.menu_desc: Second conditional and diplomatic modals to negotiate scope and deadlines -- 27 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 17 -- Negotiating & Managing Expectations
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Patrícia Xavier Lima Iwashita | Aula 17 -- Negotiating & Managing Expectations

listenings.text: Hi Patrícia, this is David, leaving a quick note before our call. Thank you for the health report — the numbers look good, and adoption is clearly up. So now my team is excited, and honestly, we would like more. We want three new features, and we want them before the next quarter. I know that is a big ask, and I understand there may be a trade-off somewhere. But this is important for us, so let us see what is realistic. If you could be flexible on the timeline, we would really appreciate it. Let us talk it through on the call and find an agreement that works for both sides. Talk soon.

Hi Patrícia, it is Sofia. Before your negotiation call, here is one thing I have learned. The goal is not to say yes to everything, and it is not to say no. The goal is to find a fair agreement. So always lead with what is realistic. If a customer asks for three features at once, I usually say: if we focused on the most important one first, we would deliver real value faster. That is a trade-off, and customers respect it. Watch out for scope creep, where the work quietly grows and grows. Be flexible, but protect the deadline. A good negotiator keeps the balance between what the customer wants and what the team can truly commit to. You will do great. Talk soon.
